chunk_summary: Structured workup for suspected rickets involves biochemical tests, radiology, and targeted evaluations to differentiate nutritional rickets from renal, endocrine, or genetic causes.
learning_objectives: Understand the biochemical markers used to diagnose nutritional rickets.
Learn the role of radiological findings in confirming rickets and monitoring treatment response.
Identify criteria for broadening evaluation to non-nutritional causes of rickets.
Recognize the importance of structured clinical pathways in managing suspected rickets.
key_facts: Initial tests include serum calcium, phosphate, alkaline phosphatase, albumin, creatinine, and magnesium (if hypocalcemia is suspected).
Classic nutritional rickets shows high alkaline phosphatase, low phosphate, and variable calcium levels.
Radiographs of the wrist or knee showing metaphyseal cupping, fraying, and widened growth plates confirm rickets.
Vitamin D deficiency is confirmed with 25(OH)D levels; secondary hyperparathyroidism is identified via PTH testing.
A correctly prescribed 12-week regimen of vitamin D and calcium should show biochemical and radiologic improvement.
reference_quote: Suspected rickets should be evaluated with a structured pathway that distinguishes nutritional rickets from renal tubular and genetic disorders. (Source: https://iapindia.org/pdf/Ch-013-Rickets.pdf)
document_key_topics: Vitamin D and calcium treatment protocols
